data.title: URNM: ETF With Global Exposure In The Uranium Industry
data.description:
Fast Facts About the Sprott Uranium Miners ETF

The Sprott Uranium Miners ETF (URNM) was launched on December 3, 2019 and tracks the North Shore Global Uranium Mining Index. URNM has 26 holdings, a trailing 12-month yield of 2.54% and an expense ratio of 0.75%. Distributions are paid annually. It is a large and liquid ETF, with $2.4 billion of AUM (assets under management) and an average daily dollar volume of $69 million. The fund’s issuer Sprott is an asset management company headquartered in Toronto, Canada, with a focus on precious metals and critical materials investment strategies. Sprott also has merchant banking and resource lending services for natural resources companies.

Underlying Index

As described in the prospectus by Sprott, the underlying index targets exchange-listed companies that have a significant portion of their operations related to uranium. The index was revised in December 2025. Its main current rules are listed below. To be included in the index, companies must have at least:

  • $125 million in market capitalization (and are excluded if they fall below $100 million).
  • $100,000 in 3-month average daily traded value (and are excluded if they fall below $75,000).

The index selects at least 25 constituents, relaxing some constraints if necessary. It is split in two parts:

  • 82.5% of asset value in miners, explorers, developers and producers.
  • 17.5% in companies holding physical uranium, uranium royalties, or other non-mining assets.

Companies are weighted separately in each part based on free-float market capitalization, with maximum limits of 20% in any constituent and 50% in aggregate in those weighting more than 5%. The index is reconstituted semi-annually and rebalanced quarterly. I will use as a benchmark a metals and mining index excluding gold and silver, represented by the iShares MSCI Global Metals & Mining Producers ETF (PICK).

URNM Portfolio

Based on company domicile, Canada is the heaviest country in the portfolio (60.5%), followed by Australia (18.3%), the U.S. (7.3%), Hong Kong (5.2%), Kazakhstan (4.8%) and the U.K. (3.9%).

The portfolio is very concentrated. Indeed, the top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 80.8% of asset value and the heaviest position weighs 21.45%. A large share of the physical uranium part is allocated to a Canada-listed closed-end fund of the same issuer: Sprott Physical Uranium Trust (U.U:CA).

URNM is in the third position for size and liquidity behind URA and NLR. It has the lowest (though very high) total return over the past 17 months. URA has the highest return, and NUKZ the best risk-adjusted performance (Sharpe ratio), due to lower volatility. Nonetheless, this time frame is too short to compare the long-term potential of these funds.

Takeaway

URNM offers global exposure in the uranium mining industry, and to a lesser extent in the physical metal, with a concentrated portfolio of 25 stocks and a closed-end fund. URNM may be used as a long-term investment, but its high volatility makes it more compelling for tactical allocation and swing trading. Nonetheless, its competitor URA may be a better alternative, with better recent performance, higher liquidity, lower turnover, better diversification and slightly lower fees.

This article answers these three main questions about URNM:

  • How is URNM’s portfolio structured?
  • How does URNM compare to an industry benchmark and peers?
  • What type of strategies and investors is URNM best suited for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: EXG: For International And Domestic Equities, Buy These Two Funds Instead
data.description:
The Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Global Diversified Equity Income Fund (EXG) is a closed-end fund designed to provide investors with exposure to domestic and international, dividend-paying equities while enhancing income through a covered call options strategy. While the strategy has historically performed well, providing investors with consistent monthly income, alternative funds may be more optimal when considering performance, fees, and portfolio diversification. In this report, I will discuss the overall theme of the fund and provide an argument for investors to consider the combination of two alternative funds, the Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) for domestic equity exposure and the Avantis International Large-Cap Value ETF (AVIV) for international exposure.

About Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Global Diversified Equity Income Fund

EXG was launched by Eaton Vance on February 27, 2007 and trades on the NYSE Arca Exchange. EXG has a modest fee of 107bps, comparable to funds like the Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund (CSQ) and SRH Total Return Fund (STEW), which have management fees of 100bps and 90bps, respectively, excluding additional fees applied to leverage. EXG is presently diversified across 95 holdings with a 32% options overlay, meaning that 32% of the total assets have covered calls applied to the positions.

When comparing funds, I believe investors must weigh their overall investment objectives and relative fees related to the funds. For example, an investor may want broad diversification across domestic & international equities, value & growth, small-, mid-, and large-cap equities, and fixed income. While EXG provides investors with both domestic and international equities, I believe performance can be enhanced segmenting out the two themes through the use of two alternative strategies.

While EXG has performed relatively well compared to peer strategies, the Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) has far outperformed peer income strategies on a total return basis over most historical holding periods all while charging a low fee of 49bps.

Though a comparison will not be all-inclusive given that EXG invests in international equities whereas ADX is primarily focused in domestic equities, the two funds have some modest overlap that may have resulted in considerable performance differences. For domestic equity exposure, I believe ADX can provide greater value to investors relative to EXG.

Looking at the top holdings, EXG has a 23.41% concentration in mega-cap technology stocks, which have experienced mixed performance in the last year. While holdings like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO) and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) have far outperformed, Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), and Apple Inc. (AAPL) have grown modestly, underperforming the broader portfolio as well as the S&P 500 (SPY).

Taking into consideration sectors, ADX has substantial greater concentration in the high-growth information technology sector whereas EXG is more broadly diversified across sectors, primarily the result of differentiated international industry prioritization.

For broader international exposure, the Avantis International Large-Cap Value ETF (AVIV) may be a more appealing comparison, though the strategy provides investors with no US exposure. I believe this factor facilitates the case for the combination of investing in ADX and AVIV as opposed to EXG given that the two funds may not have overlapping equity exposures. From a total portfolio diversification perspective, AVIV will provide investors with differentiated sector concentrations with respect to ADX, providing differentiated exposure to an investor's overall portfolio strategy.

AVIV even further underweights the information technology sector at a low portfolio weight of 3% while prioritizing old economy sectors like industrials at 18%, materials at 15%, and energy at 12%. This compares to EXG’s allocations of 12.38% to industrials, 2.42% to materials, and 2.91% to energy. Despite the lower information technology exposure, AVIV has far outperformed EXG when comparing 1-year total returns as well as since AVIV’s inception on September 28, 2021. In addition to this, AVIV has a low expense ratio of 25bps, making it an appealing alternative for international equity exposure.

Rationalizing growth for AVIV, traditional industries may exhibit growth in the coming years given their ability to apply new technologies like AI to enhance operational performance. For example, Shell plc (SHEL), the top holding in AVIV, is applying AI to improve operations using remote sensing for carbon capture as well as to enhance its research capabilities for biofuel. While I believe applying AI to business operations remains in the early stages, traditional sectors may benefit the most from improved operational efficiencies that can be reflected in share price returns. While EXG may gain exposure to this theme, I believe AVIV may provide greater exposure to the growth potential offered from companies apply AI applications.

Regionally, EXG is heavily concentrated in North American and European equities with portfolio weights of 57% and 33.25%, respectively. While this can be appealing given the high-growth-potential US information technology companies have to offer, international equities, in general, may offer investors greater value. For example, the Vanguard Total International Stock ETF (VXUS) has an aggregate price/earnings ratio of 17.5x, well below the S&P 500’s 27.12x valuation.

While valuation isn’t necessarily an indicator for future growth or performance, I believe investor sentiment towards growth stocks has been waning in recent months as the market becomes wary of the high capital costs for developing AI along with the risks involved in the high upfront investments. When comparing 6-month performance of the S&P 500 (SPY), Value (SPYV), and Growth (SPYG), we can observe the rotation in performance towards value.

EXG pays out a monthly distribution with an annualized forward rate of $0.79/share, yielding 8.62%, comparable to ADX which yields 8.21% on a forward basis.

Distributions are primarily sourced from net investment income, creating a more tax-efficient income stream for investors. This can be particularly appealing for investors on a fixed income or for those utilizing a taxable account.

Bottom Line

Compared to peer strategies, investors must weigh whether investing in a domestic and international fund is optimal for their portfolio performance, particularly when compared to pure domestic or international ex-US fund strategies. While diversification is prudent for a complete portfolio, I believe combining the expertise of multiple portfolio management teams may be more ideal given their segmented expertise in either domestic or international equities.

Comparing a portfolio of ADX and AVIV with portfolio weights of 57% and 43% to EXG [denoted as Portfolio 2], the sample portfolio provides investors greater performance, stronger annualized active returns, and relatively lower risk.

Risks Related to EXG

EXG is a diversified equity strategy designed to provide investors with enhanced total returns through tax-efficient distributions, potentially exposing investors to certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. CEFs are oftentimes less transparent in their portfolio holdings; for example, EXG last reported the portfolio makeup on January 31, 2026. While the strategy is diversified across regions and sectors, the strategy exhibits significant exposure to domestic mega-cap technology stocks that may influence the total performance of the fund.

The strategy overlays covered call options in order to enhance income, potentially capping total returns given that only 1.70% of the options are out of the money, adding risk to limiting upside potential of the strategy. EXG has underperformed relative to peer income CEFs, potentially making the strategy less optimal for investors when considering total returns.

Final Thoughts

While EXG can provide investors with diversified equity exposure with tax-efficient distributions, the fund has underperformed peer strategies, potentially making the strategy a less optimal investment opportunity. Depending on the investor’s risk tolerance, income, and tax sensitivity, I believe the sample portfolio, which combines exposure to ADX and AVIV, may be a more ideal investment strategy when compared to EXG. Given the underperformance of the fund, I am recommending EXG with a Hold rating.

data.title: KalVista Pharmaceuticals: Has Potential But With Caveats
data.description:
Shares of protease inhibitor concern KalVista Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (KALV) have moved up following FDA approval of its HAE therapy Ekterly. Since the July 2025 green light, the first oral on-demand treatment for HAE attacks has generated revenue of $49 million, already receiving patient start forms from ~20% of U.S. patient population. With likely one year plus of blue sky before an oral competitor from Pharvaris (PHVS) and a single-administration functional cure from Intellia (NTLA) hit the market, KalVista merited another look. An analysis/recommendation follows below.

When I last visited KalVista in summer 2024, its stock was trading near $13 and purchase for a trade was suggested given the assumed positive Phase 3 clinical results from (and eventual launch of) oral sebetralstat (now Ekterly) for the treatment of hereditary angioedema [HAE]. A sign to exit the trade would be positive Phase 2 data from Intellia Therapeutics’ CRISPR program Lonvo-z (then NLTA-2002) treating the same indication. [For the benefit of those that did not read the prior report, parts of it are repeated below.]

KalVista Pharmaceuticals, Inc. is a Cambridge, Massachusetts based commercial-stage biopharmaceutical concern focused on the development and marketing of small molecule protease inhibitors for diseases with significant unmet need. The company sells one commercial product, Ekterly (sebetralstat), which was approved for the on-demand treatment of HAE attacks in patients 12 years and older in July 2025. KalVista was founded in England and Wales in 2011 and went public when it completed a share purchase transaction with failed osteoarthritis company Carbylan Therapeutics in 2016 with its first trade conducted at $7.78 per share. The stock trades around $18.00 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $920 million.

Ekterly

KalVista’s raison d’etre is Ekterly, an oral plasma kallikrein (PKal) inhibitor approved in seven countries for the treatment of HAE. PKal is a serine protease enzyme that is a key early mediator of inflammation and edema. The inflammatory effects of PKal are regulated by an inhibitor protein called C1-esterase inhibitor (C1-INH). Most patients with HAE have genetic mutations that lead to a C1-INH deficiency, allowing PKal to proliferate.

Backing up, HAE is a rare and potentially life-threatening condition characterized by episodes of debilitating and painful swelling in the skin, gastrointestinal tract, or airways that afflicts ~6,500 to ~8,000 in each the U.S. and EU. On average, attack frequency is approximately once every two weeks. HAE is treated both prophylactically and ‘on-demand’ when an acute episode occurs. At ~$2.5 billion, the prophylactic market is about two and half times the on-demand market (~$1 billion), although, owing to the fact that approximately 50% of patients on preventative therapy still suffer acute episodes, it is recommended that all HAE patients carry on-demand therapy.

Before Ekterly, acute episode treatments included (and still include) Pharming Healthcare’s C1-INH Ruconest, CSL Behring’s (CSLLY) C1-INH Berinert, as well as Takeda’s (TAK) peptidomimetic Firazyr (icatibant) and PKal inhibitor Kalbitor (ecallantide) – the latter of which has a boxed warning for anaphylaxis. Generic versions of icatibant are also available. All are self-administered injectables with attendant preparation and delivery inconvenience leading to delayed or no treatment. Attack durations are ~2.75-fold shorter when treatment is delivered within one hour of attack onset, yet 60% of those who possessed the wherewithal to treat failed to do so, largely due to the administrative inconvenience. Furthermore, less than 40% of patients continually carried on-demand treatment on their person outside their home, leaving the ~$1 billion on-demand market susceptible to disruption from KalVista’s oral alternative.

With that as the treatment backdrop, Ekterly was approved on the strength of its double-blind crossover Phase 3 trial (KONFIDENT), demonstrating median time to the beginning of symptom relief of 1.61 hours (300mg, p<0.0001) and 1.79 hours (600mg, p=0.0013) versus 6.72 hours for placebo. Consistent with other trials, there were no trial discontinuations or serious adverse events. News of its approval sent shares of KALV 26% higher in the subsequent trading session in early July 2025, but they have not moved meaningfully since.

Owning patent protection until 2040, KalVista is attempting to expand Ekterly’s label to include children ages 2 to 11 via an oral disintegrating tablet that is undergoing evaluation in a Phase 3 study (KONFIDENT-KID). To date, the data are constructive, with median time to symptom relief of 1.5 hours for the 150mg dose across 65 attacks in 26 children as of a June 6, 2025 cutoff date. The therapy was well tolerated, with no adverse events or issues swallowing. Assuming no surprises, the company intends to file an sNDA in 3Q26.

Due to its delivery convenience, Ekterly sales are off to a strong start, generating revenue of ~$49 million in its two quarters on the market (2H25). A total of 1,318 patient start forms have been received in the U.S., equal to nearly 20% of all domestic HAE patients currently receiving treatment. Additionally, more than half of Ekterly’s ~$34 million in 4Q25 revenue was derived from refills.

Looming Threats

Although KalVista currently enjoys a monopoly on oral on-demand HAE treatments, it is facing three distinctive competitive threats. First, Pharvaris’ oral bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist deucrictibant achieved its primary endpoint in a Phase 3 trial (RAPID-e3) with patients 12 years and older experiencing symptom relief 1.28 hours after administration versus more than 12 hours on placebo (p<0.0001). Although comparisons across clinical trials can be challenging, those data suggest potential superiority to Ekterly. Pharvaris anticipates filing an NDA for deucrictibant in 1H26.

Second, prophylactic treatments are becoming more effective with less dosing, meaning fewer subcutaneous injections (i.e., greater compliance) and fewer attacks to be treated by on-demand therapeutics. CSL Limited’s once-monthly, subcutaneously injected Factor XIIa inhibitor Andembry (garadacimab) was approved by the FDA in June 2025 after achieving more than 99% median reduction and an 88% mean reduction in HAE attacks requiring on-demand therapy (2.07 attacks per month on placebo versus 0.22 on Andembry) in its pivotal trial (VANGUARD). That new market entrant was soon followed by Ionis Pharmaceuticals’ (IONS) once-monthly, subcutaneously injected prekallikrein-directed antisense oligonucleotide Dawnzera (donidalorsen) in August 2025, approved after it reduced monthly HAE attack rates by 81% versus placebo in its registrational study (OASIS-HAE). For those completely adverse to self-administered once-monthly needles, BioCryst’s (BCRX) oral plasma kallikrein inhibitor Orladeyo (berotralstat) is approved prophylactically for patients two years and older. That said, it is not nearly as effective as Andembry or Dawnzera.

Lastly and most importantly, Intellia Therapeutics’ CRISPER program lonvoguran ziclumeran (Lonvo-z) has the potential to disrupt the entirety of the ~$3.5 billion global HAE treatment market, prophylactic and on-demand. The one-time outpatient IV-infusion is designed to knock out the KLKB1 gene to reduce kallikrein activity at its source, which is currently undergoing evaluation in a fully enrolled 80-patient Phase 3 study (HAELO).

To date, the data on 32 infused patients across Lonvo-z’s Phase 1 and 2 trials are encouraging, with 24 patients both attack-free and long-term prophylaxis (LTP)-free for at least six months, all remaining so after most recent follow-up. Seven patients were both attack-free and LTP-free for less than six months, all remaining so after most recent follow-up. That said, four of those seven have been on treatment for less than six months. There is only one outlier, and that patient has experienced a 59% reduction in attacks from baseline over 22 months. Only one severe adverse event (pulmonary embolism) has been reported as of a August 29, 2025 cutoff date, but that patient had multiple risk factors including smoking and obesity.

Furthermore, a survey showed that 64% of HAE patients (n=104) were extremely or very likely to take Lonvo-z if prescribed (35% somewhat likely). Ninety-two percent of physicians (n=151) surveyed stated that they would prescribe Lonvo-z, if approved. It should also be noted that 76% of HAE patients exclusively taking on-demand treatments were extremely or very likely to try Lonvo-v.

Top-line data from HAELO are anticipated in mid-2026 with Illumina planning a commercial launch of Lonvo-z in 1H27.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

Without any other clinical program and serious competitive threats all around, KalVista held cash and marketable securities of $309.2 million against convertible debt of $139.0 million as of September 30, 2025. With Ekterly only on the market for two quarters, its cash burn is significant – $127.0 million for the first nine months of FY25 – likely needing $200 million in sales to breakeven. To further help with its cash needs, the company entered into a synthetic royalty agreement with DRI Healthcare Trust (DHTRF) in November 2024, under which it has received total funding of $122 million to date. In return, DRI will essentially receive 6% of Ekterly’s net sales.

Despite the looming threats from Pharvaris, prophylactics, and Intellia, the Street is unanimously positive on KalVista’s prospects with nine buy or outperform ratings and a mean price objective of roughly $35.00 a share. On average, sell-side analysts expect Ekterly to generate revenue of $186 million in FY26, steadily growing to north of $600 million in FY30.

Verdict:

If sell-side analysts are correct on Ekterly’s sales forecasts, KalVista is a wildly undervalued stock, currently priced at nearly $600 million net of net cash or one times FY30 product revenue. And arguably, it could share the oral on-demand market with Pharvaris and currently enjoys a meaningful first-mover advantage. However, the on-demand opportunity is shrinking due to effectiveness of new prophylactic therapies. But most importantly, the bet here is that Intellia has a functional cure for HAE, which will be confirmed by data in mid-2026. At that time, the clock begins running out on Ekterly (and by extension KalVista), rendering the $600 million FY30 sales forecast by the Street a mirage. For the avoidance of doubt, patients will likely carry an oral on-demand treatment for a ‘just-in-case’ moment, but the chances of it ever being deployed will be slim, meaning no refills. As such, with nothing else currently in its pipeline, I am passing on making any investment in KalVista at this time.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Europe Is 'Cheap' For A Reason: Why The 6.2x Energy Tax Means Maximum Pain For European Value Trade
data.description:
We've now seen a couple of episodes of official jawboning to calm markets. Scott Bessent came in last week to publicly address key market fears, and markets calmed. This week, a coordinated effort to talk up oil supply did the trick.

Both addressed sentiment shocks. But let's take a look where the pain is proving to be fundamentally structural, not just a shock to sentiment.

We can see it in this chart...

This shows the change in central bank policy outlook (the Fed in blue and the European Central Bank in red) since the U.S. strikes on Iran.

As you can see, the market outlook for the policy rate by the end of the year has gone up (relative to pre-war levels) for both over the past 8 trading days.

But the market expects the Fed to cut rates more than a quarter point by year-end.

In Europe, the pendulum has swung from easing to tightening. The market is now looking for more than one quarter-point hike from the ECB.

So, the two most powerful central banks in the world now have a 64-basis point divergence in rate expectations by year-end - and it's widening.

Why the U.S./Europe divergence?

The U.S. is energy-dominant (a net exporter). Europe is energy-dependent.

As we observed in this chart on Monday, due to the U.S./Iran war, Europe is now paying the equivalent of six times more than the U.S. for energy (nat gas).

This puts pressure on European fiscally fragile countries, especially those with high energy spending relative to the size of their economies (energy/GDP). Italy fits the description for both.

So the behavior in the interest rate markets (from our first chart) is about fiscal risk, not about inflation.

This is why the Italian bond yields are moving higher, faster than more fiscally sound parts of Europe. This is why the euro is down almost 2.5% against the dollar since the war. And it's why European stocks are feeling more pain than U.S. stocks.

Original Post

data.title: DICK'S Sporting Goods: Foot Locker And Oil Prices Remain Concerning Despite Q4 Beat
data.description:
Shares of Dick’s Sporting Goods (DKS) have been a mixed performer over the past year, trading close to flat. While the company has reported solid financial results and remains a strong operator, there are strategic questions still outstanding about its acquisition of Foot Locker that have weighed on sentiment. More recently, the surge in oil prices threatens to weaken discretionary consumer spending. I last covered Dick’s in November, rating shares a “sell” given my concerns about Foot Locker. Since then, the stock is down about 5%. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit DKS.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Dick’s Sporting Goods earned $3.45, which beat estimates by $0.51; revenue was up 60% but that is because this was the company’s first full quarter having ownership of Foot Locker. Foot Locker was $0.60 dilutive to EPS vs stand-alone Dick’s given the need to issue about ~10 million shares as part of the deal and the fact Foot Locker detracted about $15 million from net income. I expect Foot Locker to remain dilutive to stand-alone Dick’s at least through 2026, though the magnitude of the headwind should narrow. I continue to question the long-term benefits of this deal. As a reminder with this acquisition, Dick’s is now a global business given FL’s large international presence.

Dick's standalone performance was strong

In Q4, same-store sales growth at Dick’s was 3.1%. Impressively, operating margins were up 90bps to 11%. The company has weathered the tariff pressure quite well so far. Of course, subsequent to quarter end, the Supreme Court overturned most of the Administration’s tariffs. There are now interim 15% tariffs in place, and I expect the Administration to use other legal authority to reimpose tariffs. As such, I do not expect tariff rates to necessarily fall going forward. One outstanding question is whether refunds are issued. I expect this to be a long, drawn-out process, and for now am assuming no present value to any potential refunds, which may prove conservative.

Additionally, Dick’s has been opening new locations, including 31 last year. New stores are typically margin dilutive as it takes time for sales to ramp up, but the costs of running the store are high from day one. As these new stores enter normalized sales patterns, their pressure on margins is diminishing, enabling some of the margin accretion we are seeing.

Foot Locker struggles continue

Meanwhile, in its Foot Locker segment, same-store sales were down 3.4%. Segment operating income was -$6 million on $2.2 billion of sales. This brand has continued to struggle, an ongoing theme since before its acquisition, and this will not be an immediate fix. Since acquiring Foot Locker, Dick’s has blown out excess and unwanted inventory, which management has dubbed its “clean out the garage" plan. The hope is that by right-sizing inventories and improving product mix, the brand can be positioned for a return to growth in 2026. I remain cautious on how quickly the business can turn.

Given the acquisition of FL, inventories were up 47% from last year to $4.9 billion. This was 13% below the pace of sales growth, which suggests the company’s effort to tackle Foot Locker’s inventory problem has been largely accomplished. Too much inventory can force retailers to mark down products and sacrifice margin, so slower inventory growth than sales growth is generally an encouraging trend.

Free cash flow faces pressure, but the balance sheet is strong

Dick’s generated $400 million of free cash flow in 2025. As you can see below, that was down about $100 million from last year and by more than half from 2024. The primary driver of this decline has been Dick’s increasing cap-ex spending as it accelerates store openings and refreshes locations. In 2026, we will see cap-ex rise further as it invests in Foot Locker’s footprint to begin modernizing stores, and I expect cap-ex to run at elevated levels at least through 2027. As such, I expect free cash flow to moderate further in 2026 relative to 2025.

Even after its purchase of Foot Locker, the company maintains a solid balance sheet with $1.35 billion of cash and just $1.9 billion of debt. This allows the company to continue returning capital to shareholders. Indeed, alongside results, the board authorized a 3% dividend increase, and shares offer a 2.6% dividend yield going forward. I expect minimal share repurchases given the increase cap-ex needs.

Looking forward, the company is targeting 2-4% same-store sales growth for the legacy Dick’s business while its new Foot Locker business should grow sales 1-3%. It also expects FL to turn a small profit of $100-$150 million. I am particularly cautious on the ability to generate this level of growth at FL. Based on this, management expects to earn $13.50-$14.50 in 2026. Analyst consensus currently sits at $14.83, so this guidance is relatively soft, reflecting the ongoing challenges at Foot Locker.

Oil and FL cloud the outlook; I would avoid DKS

Dick’s outlook is disappointing relative to consensus, and I would also note consensus has been coming down. When I last covered DKS in the fall, 2026 consensus was $15.39. At that time, I was targeting ~$14.40 of 2026 earnings, so management’s outlook was more consistent with my own. This view drove my $195 price target, which is where shares were trading before releasing earnings.

Overall, I see two major questions overhanging this outlook. The first is whether Dick’s can return FL to same-store sales growth and generate a profit. The second is to what extent the surge in gasoline prices we are experiencing reduces discretionary spending. Of course, the worse the gas price situation gets the harder it will be to deliver on FL’s improvement irrespective of Foot Locker-specific performance as macro weakness can overwhelm company-specific initiatives.

Obviously, the impact of higher gasoline prices remains to be seen, and a major variable is the duration of the Iran crisis. Each day the Strait of Hormuz traffic flow remains impaired, the world likely loses over 10M barrels of oil, more than offsetting the emergency release of reserves. Higher gasoline prices serve like a tax on consumers’ income, leaving them with less to spend elsewhere at Dick’s, Foot Locker, and other retailers. I view the duration of the crisis as highly uncertain, but it is now at least a 0.3% headwind to growth this year in my view, and if the crisis extends beyond 3/31, the cost will likely grow.

All else equal, that will be a headwind for Dick’s. Beyond that, I continue to be dubious of the ability to quickly turn around FL. It has much more exposure to mall traffic than legacy Dick’s, and Dick’s has no operating history overseas. Given FL’s global footprint, this remains an execution risk. Returning FL to sales growth and reinvigorating a brand that has faced years of decline is likely to take longer in my view, and I expect same-store sales growth to be no more than 1%.

As such, I am targeting about $13.50-$14.25 in EPS this year. I see fair value in the $182-$190 area at 13-14x earnings. While shares are reacting positively to the Q4 beat and the fact guidance was not even worse, I still view the risk/return profile as unattractive especially with the company likely to have depressed free cash flow given its need to boost cap-ex. Plus, I remain concerned the market is underpricing the risk to consumer spending from the ongoing oil shock. For these reasons, I remain a sell on DKS.

data.title: Li Auto: Too Cheap To Make Sense
data.description:
After NIO (NIO) reported a staggering improvement in its profitability profile earlier this week, resulting in the first-ever operating income and net profit on a GAAP basis, Li Auto Inc. (LI) also reported better-than-expected earnings on Thursday: the EV maker beat expectations for both the bottom line and reported a sequential improvement in its vehicle margins. Despite a significant Q/Q margin boost, Li Auto lost the leading position in terms of vehicle margins to NIO in the last quarter. I still like Li Auto, however, as the company is profitable and new EV product releases in 2026 could help the EV enterprise accelerate its top line growth.

Previous rating

I previously rated shares of Li Auto a Strong Buy -- Potential To Double -- mainly because the EV company traded, unjustifiably in my opinion, below the industry group average price-to-revenue ratio. The main appeal for Li Auto is its strong margin picture as well as its considerable top line growth potential. In addition, the return to profitability in Q4'25 and new product releases could help the EV company catch up to its electric vehicle rivals.

Li Auto beat on earnings in Q4 '25

The EV enterprise beat analyst expectations on the bottom line, but missed on the top line on Thursday: it reported $0.04 per-share in non-GAAP EPADS vs. $0.03 per-share expected. Li Auto also reported $4.1B in total revenue, falling short of the average revenue estimate of $4.21B.

Li Auto is servicing the new energy vehicle market in China with a specialized line-up of extended-range and battery-powered electric vehicles. In the most recent quarter, the EV platform published total revenue of 28.8B Chinese yuan ($4.1B), down 35% year-over-year.

While the company suffered a downturn in deliveries due to disappointing sales for the Li Mega (discussed further below), the EV enterprise managed to swing back to a profit on a net income basis in the fourth quarter, mainly due to aggressive cost cuts. In the fourth quarter, Li Auto reported net income of 20.2M Chinese yuan ($2.9M), highlighting a significant sequential improvement.

The main reason for the drop in revenues and deliveries was that the EV company, which is focused on the premium SUV market, is facing heavy competition from companies like BYD and Huawei-backed AITO. Further, Li Auto's new multi-purpose vehicle, the Li Mega, has seen weak sales in 2025, adding to the down-trend in deliveries. Li Auto cut staff last year in response to weaker-than-expected customer interest in the Li Mega MPV, which was also the reason why the company swung to a loss in the third quarter of 2025.

In the fourth quarter, Li Auto delivered a total of 109,194 EVs to its customers, showing a year-over-year drop-off rate of 31.2%. The trend is significantly worse than the one for NIO which reported a total delivery volume of 124,807 units in the fourth-quarter, up 71.7% year-over-year.

One of the most important financial metrics for electric vehicle makers, especially in the EV start-up group, is vehicle margins. They determine or show investors how much money companies earn on the actual production of their EV products.

In the fourth quarter, Li Auto published a vehicle margin of 16.8%, showing a Q/Q increase of 1.3 PP. NIO, which already published results on Tuesday, reported a vehicle margin of 18.1% for the fourth-quarter which establishes the EV company now at the very top of the EV start-up group in terms of margin profitability. XPeng (XPEV), which has not yet reported results for the fourth fiscal quarter, had a vehicle margin of 13.1% in the third quarter.

Li Auto’s guidance for Q1 ’26

The electric vehicle company submitted its outlook for the first fiscal quarter which calls for 85,000 to 90,000 deliveries in the first fiscal quarter, which implies a year-over-year decline of ~5% at the midpoint of guidance.

According to recent delivery updates, the company has already delivered approximately 54,000 vehicles in the months of January and February, meaning it has already achieved ~62% of its delivery guidance. Since March is traditionally a robust quarter for Chinese EV companies -- deliveries and sales typically strongly rebound due to the conclusion of Chinese New Year holidays in February -- I have a high degree of confidence that Li Auto will be able to meet or surpass its delivery guidance range for Q1 '26.

Depressed valuation multiplier...

Li Auto is facing some issues in its business with the unenthusiastic market response to the Li Mega MPV, but the valuation factor is nonetheless disconnected from the overall strong economic reality of this start-up EV play. Shares of Li Auto are currently trading at a price-to-revenue ratio of 0.71X, which makes the EV enterprise the second-cheapest play in the industry group after NIO. XPeng is the highest-valued EV company and is priced at close to a ~1.0X forward P/S ratio, mainly because of soaring delivery growth, improving vehicle margins and the sales success of the X9 EREV.

In my opinion, Li Auto could revalue to a P/S ratio of ~1.0X given the firm’s solid profitability, potential for a reversal in delivery growth and respectable guidance for the first-quarter. A 1.0X P/S ratio implies a fair value for Li Auto in the neighborhood of $25.40 per-share, which calculates to about 40% upside revaluation potential.

Risks with NIO

While Li Auto faces some challenges in its core EV division, the company is still doing well in terms of vehicle margins... and actually managed to improve its margins on a sequential basis. Li Auto also swung back to a (small) profit in Q4 '25 which reduces risks for investors. What would change my mind about Li Auto is if the EV company saw a deteriorating margin trend. or if new models, which are set for launch in Q2 '26, such as the all-new Li L9 SUV, receive a muted market reception as well.

Final thoughts

Li Auto submitted a mixed earnings report card for the fourth fiscal quarter on Thursday that missed on revenues and showed significant top line and delivery pressure. The outlook for Q1 '26 was decent, given the firm's challenges, and Li Auto expects up to 90,000 unit deliveries in the first-quarter. On the positive side, Li Auto beat EPADS estimates and reported a sequential improvement in its vehicle margin.

What I like about Li Auto is that the company's shares trade at a depressed valuation multiplier and new product releases could help the company reaccelerate its growth momentum in Q2 '26. With new products such as the Li L9 full-size luxury SUV coming to market soon, Li Auto has a catalyst in its business to return its delivery and top line growth to positive territory.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Amgen: MariTide And Osteoporosis Sales Fuel The Rally
data.description:
In my last article, "Can Amgen Sustain Its Rally In 2026", I focused mainly on Amgen's Q3 results (AMGN) and the efficacy of MariTide in the Phase 2 trial [NCT05669599].

I also talked about the reasons why, in my opinion, soft demand for its osteoporosis franchise's flagship, called Prolia [denosumab], is no longer creating considerable pressure on AMGN stock.

But what exciting things have happened in the last few months? And why am I continuing to cover Amgen with a 'Buy' rating even though its shares have risen 19.1% over the past four months?

And first, I will note the dry numbers.

Its revenue rose 8.6% year-over-year to $9.87 billion, beating Wall Street analysts' expectations by $406.1 million.

Meanwhile, Amgen's non-GAAP EPS also came in well above the consensus forecast, as in the past six quarters, but this time by $0.56.

Also, on February 12, the EC approved Uplizna [inebilizumab] for the treatment of certain people suffering from a neuromuscular disorder called gMG [generalized myasthenia gravis].

And, given the article in PMC, its prevalence ranges from 150 to 200 cases per million people in Europe.

By the way, sales of Uplizna were $233 million for the three months ended December 31, up 130.7% YoY and also beat my 'base case' scenario by $23 million, despite competition from AstraZeneca's Soliris/Ultomiris (AZN) and argenx's Vyvgart/Vyvgart Hytrulo (ARGX).

I believe the strong performance of this CD19-directed cytolytic antibody is primarily due to increased demand for the treatment of IgG4-RD patients, as well as its approval on December 11 last year for gMG.

Now, let's talk about Amgen's osteoporosis franchise.

Evenity offsets the decline in demand for denosumab

As you may know, Amgen is one of the leaders in the metabolic bone diseases market, but the loss of exclusivity for Prolia/Xgeva in early 2025 has weakened its position.

And if I look at the current situation, the FDA has already approved seven biosimilars to this 'duo', four of which have already been launched.

However, I believe that among these, only Sandoz's Jubbonti/Wyost (SDZNY) is scaling fast enough to capture denosumab's share in the RANKL inhibitors market.

And, primarily due to this copycat, net revenues of denosumab are down 13% year-on-year and 10.5% QoQ to $1.5 billion in Q4, which is only $25 million lower than my 'best case' scenario.

But as we all know, Big Pharma, especially Amgen, doesn't want to lose even a piece of this market, estimated to be worth $19.7 billion by 2032.

So Amgen is actively developing a successor, jointly with UCB, to Prolia/Xgeva called Evenity [romosozumab].

To my regret, the FDA's Purple Book contains no data about its compound/use patent protection. Only in Amgen's 10-K did I find that Evenity's US patent covering "methods of treatment" expires on April 9, 2033.

So, to put it simply, I don't expect its biosimilars to be launched for at least the next seven years.

But is Evenity's performance sufficient to offset the decline in sales of Prolia/Xgeva?

Briefly, I'll say this: yes.

So, in Q4, romosozumab generated $599 million in revenues for Amgen, up $168 million year-over-year.

In my opinion, this growth reflects AMGN's active switching of patients from denosumab to Evenity.

And secondly, on April 11 last year, UCB presented promising clinical data on this anti-sclerostin antibody at the WCO-IOF-ESCEO.

It again confirmed its efficacy in improving lumbar spine bone mass and reducing the risk of fractures in naïve patients with postmenopausal osteoporosis.

And, during the Q4 earnings call, EVP Murdo Gordon was quite enthusiastic about Evenity's prospects.

In the U.S., EVENITY sales grew 41%, driven by higher volumes from both established and new prescribers.

EVENITY leads the bone builder segment with over 60% market share and is now growing faster than the category overall. To date, approximately 300,000 U.S. patients have been treated with EVENITY with a 33% increase of new patients in just one year.

Increased investment has helped accelerate this growth, which we expect to continue. Despite strong progress, nearly 90% of the two million women at very high risk of fracture remain untreated, presenting a clear opportunity for EVENITY to drive growth and impact.

But this information is already well-known. What about romosozumab's label expansion in the next two years?

In mid-2027, I expect results from a Phase 3 study [NCT05972551].

In it, Amgen evaluates Evenity's efficacy in treating children and adolescents over five years old with osteogenesis imperfecta. Although this is a genetic disorder, it is fairly common, with an estimated incidence of 5-10 cases per 100,000.

And given romosozumab's high efficacy in the Phase 3 FRAME [NCT01575834 ] and ARCH trials, I believe its label will be expanded for this indication.

Amgen acquired Dark Blue Therapeutics

Of course, before I get into the "Risks," I should discuss its acquisition of Dark Blue Therapeutics, which was announced on January 6.

With this deal valued at up to $840 million, Amgen expanded its pipeline of experimental anti-cancer drugs with an MLLT1/3-targeted protein degrader.

But, currently, there is no data on its efficacy or toxicity profile, even in Phase 1.

So I was curious to see how attractive this mechanism of action is.

MLLT1 and MLLT3 are part of the so-called SEC [super elongation complex] and are key regulators of it.

This complex, in turn, may activate the expression of leukemogenic genes, HOXA9 and MYC, which drive AML cell growth.

The article published in the ASH also showed that blocking MLLT1/3 leads to tumor regression in several mouse models.

By the way, I wanted to find the number of acute myeloid leukemia cases in WHO's Cancer Tomorrow.

To my great regret, it doesn't provide figures only for this subtype, but even without that, it's clear that the TAM is big, as about 800,699 cases of leukemia [459.4 thousand men and 341.3 thousand women] will be diagnosed in 2050, up 64.3% from 2022.

Risks

I found several risks, such as the ongoing U.S. and Israeli operations in Iran, and Enbrel sales fell 47.6% YoY to $532 million in Q4.

I also didn't forget to note the drop in Amgen's EBIT to 30.5%, mainly due to active patient recruitment for global Phase 3 MariTide studies.

Takeaway

MariTide is getting closer to becoming a competitor to Eli Lilly's tirzepatide (LLY).

And although Amgen hasn't announced any additional clinical data, as expected, SVP Kave Niksefat mentioned once again its competitive advantages.

We remain confident in MariTide's potential as a differentiated treatment for obesity, type 2 diabetes and obesity-related conditions.

In a field featuring dozens of potential daily oral and weekly injectable medicines, MariTide stands alone as the only therapy in late-stage development to offer the paradigm-changing prospect of strong efficacy and favorable tolerability at monthly, every other month or even quarterly dosing.

But MariTide is still an experimental drug. Besides Uplizna and Evenity, which I discussed earlier, in Q4 I was again pleased to see a strong performance from its cholesterol lowering blockbuster, Repatha.

Its sales grew 9.6% quarter-on-quarter to $870 million, mainly due to its FDA approval on August 25 last year for the treatment of certain patients with increased risk for major adverse cardiovascular events and HoFH, a rare form of familial hypercholesterolemia.

As a result, I continue to cover Amgen with a 'Buy' rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Banc Of California: High Yielding Preferred Stock With A Likely Call In 2027
data.description:
Introduction

I’m keeping an eye on the performance of the Banc of California (BANC) as although I currently only own the preferred stock, I fear that issue may be called this summer and I could perhaps be interested to recycle the proceeds and initiate a long position in the common shares. As those common shares have now lost in excess of 15% of their price in the past month, I wanted to have a look at how attractive the common shares are right now.

A look at the FY 2025 results

Looking at the bank’s full-year results, Banc of California reported a total interest income of $1.68B, a decrease of approximately $150M compared to FY 2024, while the interest expenses decreased by almost $200M. This ultimately resulted in a $51M increase in the net interest income, which was helpful to help absorb the higher loan loss provisions, which increased by approximately $28M.

The net non-interest expenses were almost $600M, which resulted in a pre-tax income of $313M and a net profit of $229M. As the income statement above shows, Banc of California needed just under $40M to cover the preferred dividends, resulting in a net income of $189.2M attributable to the common shareholders. This represents an EPS of $1.18. This means the common dividend, which was recently increased by 20%, remains well-covered.

From the perspective of a preferred shareholder, I’m of course very satisfied to see that the bank needed less than 15% of its net income to cover the preferred dividends.

Interestingly, the bank is now expecting the net interest income in 2026 to increase by 10-12% versus the 2025 result, and this is without expecting any rate cuts by the Federal Reserve. The expenses will increase by a low to mid single digit percentage, which should result in a 20-25% increase in the pre-provision income.

The 2025 pre-tax and pre-provision income was approximately $380M, and a 20% increase would indicate a $76M increase. So even if the loan loss provisions would increase, the bank will likely post a very robust earnings increase. The total amount of loans increased by just $20M at the end of 2025 compared to year-end 2024, indicating the bank still has a good grip on the quality of its loan book.

And even if I’d use a higher loan loss provision of $100M (which is arbitrary), the $76M in additional pre-tax and pre-provision income would add $46M to the pre-tax income, and approximately $30-35M to the net income. This would push the EPS to around $1.40.

The preferred stock is still interesting, but I anticipate a call this year

In a previous article, I mentioned I was starting to build a position in the bank’s Series F ( BANC.PR.F) non-cumulative preferred shares (the bank does not have to make the preferred shareholder whole for missed preferred dividend payments) which pays a 7.75% fixed preferred dividend. The $1.9375 per share is paid in four equal quarterly installments. The preferred stock is now trading at $25.16, resulting in a current yield of around 7.7%.

While that’s attractive, keep in mind these securities are callable from September 2027 on. And based on the language used on the quarterly conference calls, it sounds like the BANC management is very keen on removing these preferred securities from its capital structure. By retiring the $500M in preferred stock, the EPS on the common shares would see an uplift of approximately $0.26-0.27 per share.

On the most recent Q4 call, the bank’s CEO mentioned the preferred stock is ‘maturing next year’. That’s not true; the preferred stock is callable, but does not have a defined maturity date. But it shows how the Banc of California’s management thinks about its preferred stock, and I think it is very likely to see the bank calling the preferred securities in September 2027.

This means I am considering an investment in the preferred shares Series F like a term investment. My base case scenario uses a call on the very first call date, resulting in a yield to call of approximately 7.3%. And with the 2 year US treasury rate currently at around 3.64%, the mark-up is approximately 366 bps, which I think offers a pretty good risk/reward ratio.

Investment thesis

Based on the 2026 guidance and assuming a very conservative loan loss provision rate, the EPS should increase to $1.40. And if the loan loss provisions could remain stable (versus the 40% increase in my conservative scenario), we are looking at an EPS of $1.55. Should the Banc of California indeed redeem its preferred shares in 2027, we may see an additional uplift towards an EPS of $1.80 in 2028 (assuming no further net interest income growth in 2027 and 2028).

For now, I still just have the preferred shares, which are still a buy given the 7.3% annualized return, assuming a call in September 2027. But after taking a step back and looking at the bigger picture, the anticipated earnings growth in 2026 and 2027 in combination with the reduction of $40M in preferred dividends will very likely push the EPS to a run rate of $1.80 by the end of 2027 the latest. This also means the bank has a good shot at generating an EPS north of $2 in 2028, as it continues to retain the majority of its earnings on the balance sheet, resulting in additional growth.

This means the common shares of BANC also are a ‘buy’ at the current levels. I have a 2-3 year investment horizon for those common shares. While I currently have no position, I may be looking to initiate a long position in the next few weeks.

data.title: Lumentum's AI Optics Inflection Is No Longer Subtle
data.description:
Investment Thesis

First and foremost, let me say that Lumentum Holdings (LITE) is sitting right at the center of two forces that normally do not coincide. The first one is a structural AI driven demand wave and the second is company specific execution. And for the latter, it is already evidenced by the recent 2Q 2026 Earnings report. In the recent quarter, the company delivered $665.5Mn of revenue with a 42% non-GAAP gross margin. I would call that impressive, given that it also guided that 3Q 2026 revenue would range between $780 and $830Mn.

Secondly, I would not say that the demand signals are abstract. LITE's management is explicitly pointing to optical circuit switches [OCS] and co-packaged optics [CPO] as substantial opportunities. I say this because the OCS backlog has been described well beyond $400Mn and also an incremental multi hundred million dollar CPO order deliverable in H1 2027. And this is what I would call demand with receipts.

Now, if I just zoom out to the ecosystem I note a couple of things. For instance McKinsey's report on networking optics argues that data centers expansion and AI workloads are pulling hyperscalers toward 800Gbps and 1.6Tbps optical transceivers. The report also mentions of potential supply shortfalls especially when it comes to those high bandwidth transceivers for years. Now, that kind of environment usually rewards the companies that can ship. And this is why Lumentum is clearly pushing product volume mix and margin at the same time.

Then finally, 'adjacent players wins feeding into revenue' is not a theory. By this I am talking about the Nvidia (NVDA) strategic partnership with LITE which includes an NVDA multibillion purchase commitment. And this is in addition to a $2Bn investment to expand capacity and deepen R&D collaboration in data center optics. In my view, this is a direct line from the AI platform leader to Lumentum's order book and capex runway.

Now that I have discussed the reasons why I am rating this as a Strong Buy, let's now try to understand what the business actually is.

Business Overview

Lumentum is an optical and photonics company that sells lasers, modules and optical subsystems that are used in data center connectivity, telecom networks and industrial manufacturing and sensing. LITE describes itself as powering networks and infrastructure behind AI, cloud computing and next generation communications with global R&D manufacturing and sales. And when it comes from a segment point of view, Lumentum reports exposure to Cloud & Networking [C&N] and industrial Tech. In 2025 C&N was the largest segment. This matters because traditionally, investors have viewed LITE and anchored on the old telecom optics and cyclical lasers story. All this while I believe the current setup is far more and AI led.

Now, lets try and understand what will drive its growth in future.

Growth Drivers

The simplest way to understand the growth setup around LITE is to look at what the management highlighted as the engine in 2Q 2026. Cloud and AI drove the total company step-up. And this is because the company posted record shipments in key laser categories. Yet it is scaling its systems like cloud transceivers and OCS.

What I like about LITE is that multiple growth are contributing at once. And they don't all depend on the same customer behaviour. Optical switches are moving from interesting tech into revenue producing deployments. For instance, the 2Q 2026 report points out that the OCS shipments exceeded a $10Mn quarterly run and that manufacturing readiness is proceeding ahead of plan. The bottomline is that Lumentum remains on track for manufacturing expansion to support future growth.

I note that Q3 guidance implies another step up in operating margin to between 30% and 31%. That is exactly the type of margin curve that you see when higher value products scale and utilization improves. And this is where the 'adjacent players' point that I mentioned earlier really hits. NVDA's partnership is explicit. And this is because of the multiyear strategic agreements, a multibillion purchase agreement commitment and a $2Bn investment to expand Lumentum capacity. This is in addition to a US based manufacturing for data center optics. In my opinion, you do not need to squint to see the flywheel. NVDA grows AI factories. And AI factories need optical scaling. And Lumentum feeds that optical scaling.

To make growth profile even more concrete, the company scores an excellent A+ growth grade while showing a 48% y/y revenue growth and 51% FWD revenue growth. Those are aggressive numbers, yes. They also match the tone of management guidance and the Q3 step-up implied by the company outlook.

Now that I have discussed the growth drivers, I will now move on to discuss valuation.

Valuation

Let me start by saying that the debate for Lumentum is going to trigger 2 different instincts. And this of course depends on what you anchor on. And I say this because if the anchor is on absolute multiples, then definitely the stock is quite expensive as shown below.

But here's the other side. Premium multiples can be rational when earnings are at the start of a step-change. And LITE's recent financial trajectory looks like that. Non-GAAP operating margin moved to 25.2% in 2Q 2026 and the company guided between 30% to 31% operating margin in 3Q 2026. This means that if the denominator is expanding fast enough, then today's expensive multiple compresses on its own without multiple re-rating.

The key valuation metric from the above snapshot, and also the one I'm leaning on most is the FWD Non-GAAP PEG ratio. LITE's FWD non-GAAP PEG is 0.88x which is at a 30% discount to the sector median of 1.27x. This is direct evidence that the market is paying for Lumentum's AI optics narrative but not paying enough relative to the growth rates embedded in the forward outlook. And the forward outlook is not just a spreadsheet forecast. It is actually supported by real demand markers. OCS backlog well beyond $400Mn and a multi hundred million dollar CPO order for H1 of 2027.

When I compare it with some of the peers, I note that LITE is still at an attractive FWD PEG. For instance, Coherent Corp (COHR) trades at a FWD PEG of 1.47x, while Fabrinet (FN) has its TTM PEG at 3.01x and Lam Research (LRCX) trades at 2.09x.

Lumentum's Key Risk

The biggest risk I see for Lumentum is slowing down of AI and cloud capex. Should hyperscalers slow down, pause or shift their AI architecture, then this could hurt LITE's revenue growth. I also note that LITE has a high customer concentration rate. This means that if one or two hyperscalers adjust their build plans, then LITE will definitely feel the pinch.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, this is what I see. Lumentum is not selling a story. It is selling a product into a real bottleneck in AI infrastructure. I mean the company has the kind of operating leverage that changes how the market values a business. And when you layer that on top of OCS backlog that I had talked about previously, incremental CPO orders and Strong AI optics supply and a NVDA partnership, then it's definitely hard for me to land anywhere else. And that is why I believe Lumentum warrants a Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Unusual Machines: From Vast Opportunity To Concrete Execution (Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction: Upgrade from Hold to Strong Buy

In my June 2025 analysis of Unusual Machines (UMAC), I recognized the vast market demand for small drones against the backdrop of higher defense spending as well as the trend of using unmanned aerial vehicles for warfare. However, I merely gave a Hold rating since revenue had not been reflecting this trend and expenditures remained high, which makes me cautiously optimistic.

The latest earnings call showed that the Company is at a pivotal point, with revenue almost doubled at $4.9 million. Quoting the CEO's analogy: 2025 was loading the fuel in the rocket, but 2026 is the year of launch.

With the change of fundamentals, we are upgrading our thesis that, Unusual Machines has shown its execution quality and has shifted from a retail shop to an industrial powerhouse that has more than 80% of revenue from the B2B/B2G segment.

The regulatory moat

In December 2025, the Federal Communications Commission of the United States added the Uncrewed Aircraft Systems (UAS) and its critical components to its cover list, which aims to exert more control of technologies like drones to minimize the risks to national security. This prohibits drones and components that are produced outside the U.S. from being imported and operating in the country.

This arbitrary "moat" of domestic drone producers essentially removed major global competitors in both commercial and industrial drones from the U.S. market, like T-Motor and DJI. According to the earnings call, this created a "marketplace vacuum" in the market that the Company operates in, opening up "at least a $3 billion non-defense components marketplace" that domestic solutions will dominate within the next 3 to 5 years as older fleets retire.

What's more, in January 2026, the FCC updated its Blue UAS Cleared List, which aims to reinforce domestic supremacy by pushing drone products to have at least 65% of domestic components used. This has given UMAC a head start since multiple of its products are already in the framework, including the Rotor Riot Brave 55A ESC and Brave F7 flight controller.

According to the latest earnings results and how the CEO describes it, the regulatory shift drove its enterprise sales to more than 80% of the revenue mix from a mere 30% before the enforcement, because of its FCC/NDAA-compliant domestic supply chain. Gross margin also rose to 36% in Q4, as they are the largest domestic producer in several component categories, rising from 24% in Q1.

The $1 billion Pentagon backing

It has been obvious that this year we are in a "defense supercycle", driven mainly by the administration's pro-defense policies, actions, and, most importantly, spending. This provides a nice tailwind to UMAC, given also the Drone Dominance Program from the Department of War.

The program is worth $1.1 billion, funded by the "Big Beautiful Bill", aiming to rapidly develop and field low-cost, unmanned attack drones at scale. Given the importance of drones in modern warfare as exemplified in the Russia-Ukraine war and Iran war, the DoW plans to procure more than 300,000 small drones over the two years.

  • Gauntlet 1 (Feb–July 2026): 12 vendors selected to produce 30,000 units at an average price of $5,000 per drone.
  • Gauntlet 4 (Final Phase): Procurement increases to 150,000 units as the unit price target drops to $2,300.

For the record, more than half of the announced winners of Drone Dominance are already customers of ours in some form or fashion

- UMAC CEO Allan Evans

From the CEO of UMAC, of the 11 companies that qualified for the $150 million in delivery orders under Gauntlet 1, at least six are existing UMAC component customers. Therefore, this shows the importance of UMAC in support of this program, not as a competitor to the selected candidates, but as a necessary supplier. The average revenue potential of UMAC components in each drone under the program ranges from $500 (analyst estimates) to $1000 (management estimates), which translates into a $90 million opportunity in 2026 and $250 million in 2027.

Realizing the guided revenue requires operational readiness. To show that they can fulfill the demand, UMAC is in the transitional process to an automated motor production line in mid-2026, which will raise output volume to well over 100,000 monthly, as mentioned in the earnings call.

Financials: Recovery and significant growth in sight

The latest earnings present a bright picture of its future potential, from a piecemeal drone producer to a high-volume critical supplier of a technology that is of national security. Quarterly revenue reached $4.9 million, while total 2025 revenue totaled $11.2 million, both of which represent triple-digit growth driven by the pivot into enterprise sales that are supported by government spending - as enterprise sales % in the revenue mix increased from 31% in quarter 1 to 81% in quarter 4.

Given the discussed expansion in government spending, I estimate that the total revenue in 2026 can reach about $30 million (in line with consensus estimates) - which is one-third of the projected market from the DoW program, as the manufacturing capability of motor will reach 100,000 in the second half of 2026 and camera production will be online towards the end of the year.

More importantly, the Company is achieving the growth profitably. Its Q4 gross margin has shown improvement compared to earlier in the year. This is expected to continue in the next year once the scaling effect clicks in with the automated production.

A common worry for growth stock is its financial health. However, UMAC has shown an even more impressive performance in its management of capital and financing. The company closed 2025 with $157.4 million in the bank and no debt. This ensures the company is not constrained by resources to scale up in the face of higher demands.

Valuation

Amongst most small drone peers, UMAC is valued the lowest due to its size and the role in the drone supply chain (component-focused vs FPV / end-product). However, this latest earning shows that the company is no longer an "emerging player" for the future, but a mature supplier. As such, it is justifiable that the valuation multiple expand slightly from the current level. Therefore, the bull and bear case of valuation would be:

Bull Case Bear Case
Revenue ($m) 30 25
P/S 38x (10% from current level) 36x (5% from current level)
Target price (2026) $30 (50% upside) $24 (20% upside)

Investment risks

  • Execution over market tailwinds: The risk profile of the Company, as it matures and scales up, shifts from whether the market has demands for their products, to whether the management can scale up production fast enough and whether they can manage financials and expenditures well
  • Supply chain bottlenecks: The CEO has mentioned in the earnings call that the supply of key components like barometers has been constrained due to the sequential growth of the production and demand. With several wars happening in which drones are key, supply chain would be a key bottleneck
  • Operational integration: The risk of managing a workforce that grew nearly 10x in the past year and the technical challenge of the automated production make financial projection difficult to achieve

Conclusion

Unusual Machines has been considered a small, retail drone manufacturer and supplier in the past. However, with the backdrop of higher spending and focus on drones as a modern warfare, the Company has benefited and is playing a critical role in the industry. Together with the improving operational capability and solid financials, I believe it is time we upgrade the rating for the Company because of its concrete execution.
